data.title: Anthropic launches new Claude models for reasoning, AI agents
data.description:
Anthropic unveiled its next generation of Claude models called Claude Opus 4 and Claude Sonnet 4 for coding, advanced reasoning, and AI agents.

Anthropic — which is backed by Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) Google — said Claude Opus 4 is the world’s best coding model, with sustained performance on complex, long-running tasks and agent workflows.

The company added that Claude Sonnet 4 is a significant upgrade to Claude Sonnet 3.7, delivering superior coding and reasoning while responding more precisely to instructions.

Besides the models, the company also announced other updates. Extended thinking with tool use (beta): both models can use tools, such as web search, during extended thinking, allowing Claude to alternate between reasoning and tool use to improve responses.

Both models can use tools in parallel, follow instructions more precisely, and, when given access to local files by developers, show improved memory capabilities, according to the company.

Anthropic added that Claude Code is now generally available. In addition, the company is releasing four new capabilities on the Anthropic API that enable developers to build more powerful AI agents: the code execution tool, MCP connector, Files API, and the ability to cache prompts for up to one hour.

Anthropic noted that pricing remains consistent with previous Opus and Sonnet models: Opus 4 at $15/$75 per million tokens (input/output) and Sonnet 4 at $3/$15.

Both models are available on the Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, and Google Cloud's Vertex AI, as per the company.

Anthropic said Claude Opus 4 is its most powerful model yet and the best coding model in the world, leading on SWE-bench (72.5%) and Terminal-bench (43.2%). SWE-bench is a benchmark for performance on real software engineering task.

The company noted that it has significantly reduced behavior where the models use shortcuts or loopholes to complete tasks. Both new models are 65% less likely to engage in this behavior than Sonnet 3.7 on agentic tasks that are mainly susceptible to shortcuts and loopholes.

Claude Opus 4 also outperforms all previous models on memory capabilities, as per Anthropic.

Anthropic competes with several heavyweights in the AI space, including Google's Gemini and Microsoft (MSFT)-backed OpenAI's GPT-4.

Earlier this week, Alphabet's CEO Sundar Pichai unveiled several AI updates for Gemini at its annual developer conference Google I/O and noted that Gemini 2.5 Pro beats out every competitor in the metrics measured by LMArena. Last month, OpenAI launched GPT-4.5, its largest and most conversational model to date, as it was trained across multiple data centers. Meta Platforms (META) has its latest Llama 4 family of open-source large language models, or LLMs.

Chinese companies are not far behind, with Baidu showcasing its AI models Ernie 4.5 Turbo and new reasoning model called Ernie X1 Turbo last month. Alibaba also has AI models, with the latest being Qwen 3, which is an enhanced AI model featuring hybrid reasoning capabilities. China's DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which rattled the U.S. tech sector with its low cost AI models, had introduced its updated V3 model in March.

data.title: SA Asks: How can Target right the ship?
data.description:
On Wednesday, Target (NYSE:TGT) cut its full-year financial guidance after issuing a weaker-than-expected Q1 earnings report that reflected a significant decline in comparable store sales and market share losses to competitors. 

Reaction to the report was swift, with investors pushing shares 5% lower Wednesday. The stock is now down 31% year-to-date. In comparison, shares of rivals Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) are up 7% and 12%, respectively, from the beginning of the year.

How can Target (NYSE:TGT) right the ship to woo back customers and investors? We asked Seeking Alpha analyst Julia Ostian for her thoughts. 

Julia Ostian: To fix itself, Target (TGT) has to stop pretending this is just a difficult retail environment and start owning its structural problems.  

First, it has to bring people back into stores, and to do it fast. Target needs to lower prices on must-have products that people shop for every week, so more customers choose to come to Target (TGT) instead of going somewhere cheaper. It can't stay afloat on just offering special deals or new designer collections. This is not relevant in uncertain times.  

Second, it can’t rely on things like legal wins to make earnings look better. Sales at existing stores are falling, profit margins are getting squeezed, and online growth isn’t enough to save the whole business. Target (TGT) needs to focus more on essentials like groceries and household basics because that’s what shoppers are buying right now.  

The new “Acceleration Office” sounds promising, but it needs to actually move fast. If Target (TGT) doesn’t make real changes soon, the stock won’t just stay cheap, it will continue to go further down with a good reason.

  • Top Consumer Staples Merchandise Retail Stocks
  • Top Food Retail Stocks

data.title: If U.S. tariffs settle to 10%, the the Federal Reserve can start cutting, Waller says
data.description:
If trade deals can bring the average U.S. tariff to 10% by July, then the Federal Reserve could be in a good position to cut rates in the second half of the year, Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said on Thursday during a media interview.

"If we can get the tariffs down closer to 10%, and then that's all sealed, done and delivered somewhere by July, then we're in good shape for the second half of the year," Waller said in an interview on Fox Business's Mornings with Maria program.  "Then we're in a good position as a Fed to kind of move with rate cuts through the second half of the year."

He said the Fed is likely to remain on hold during the summer. "We're going to wait and see what these trade deals do. And we're going to have to wait and see what happens with the April 2nd tariffs that were postponed until July," he added.

Waller also said he's more optimistic now than he was a month ago that tariffs will settle to a more manageable rate.

"Every CEO I've talked to said they could deal with a 10% tariff," he said. "They can't deal with 25 very easily without passing that through or stopping other things."

The uncertainty about tariff rates, meanwhile, is causing businesses to delay investments. "The key thing is that they're not canceling anything. They're just pausing it until they get better clarity on what's going to happen," Waller said.

So far, he's viewing the tariffs as a one-time price increase, which means it won't cause persistent inflation. With that kind of price increase, the standard central bank playbook is to look through the hike. "You don't react to it because [then, you'd] raise rates, hurt the economy for something that's just a one-time price effect."

He's hearing from businesses that a 10% tariff would likely be one-third eaten by the firm, one third by the supplier, and the rest passed on to customers. He expects that might increase inflation to 2.8% from 2.5%, since imports are 10% of the price index.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Lennox announces $1B increase to existing share buybacks program
data.description:
Cooling, heating, indoor air quality, and refrigeration systems provider Lennox International (NYSE:LII) announced on Thursday a $1B increase to its existing stock repurchase authorization.

The repurchases may be made from time to time at the management's discretion through open market purchases, accelerated share repurchases, or other privately negotiated transactions.

The prior stock repurchase authorizations had ~$290M remaining as of May 12.

At the same time, the company declared a $1.30/share quarterly dividend, a 13% increase from the prior dividend of $1.15.

data.title: Snowflake's snowball effect leads enterprise software stocks higher
data.description:
Snowflake's (NYSE:SNOW) first quarter financial results prompted the stock to jump more than 10% by noon trading on Thursday, creating a snowball effect as fellow enterprise software stocks followed suit.

Shares rose after the cloud-based data platform beat first quarter expectations and raised its fiscal 2026 product revenue outlook.

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR), a producer of artificial intelligence-powered software and analytics for government agencies and enterprises, saw its shares increase nearly 4% by early afternoon trading. On Wednesday, the Denver-based company was awarded a $795M contract by the U.S. Army to work on its Maven Smart System software licenses.

Maven Smart System is an AI tool used by the U.S. Department of Defense designed to modernize battlefield operations by processing imagery and full-motion video from drones. The new contract builds significantly on the $99M contract Palantir was awarded back in September to work on the system.

Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) inched up 0.7%, after it was announced the enterprise information technology solutions company was collaborating with Nvidia (NVDA), OpenAI, Cisco Systems (NASDAQ:CSCO), SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) and G42 to build Stargate UAE. Cisco was up 0.2%.

Zoom Communications (NASDAQ:ZM) was up 0.4% following a solid first quarter earnings report.

ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) and Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) shares both edged up about 0.2%, while Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) ticked up 0.7%. SAP (NYSE:SAP) was up 0.4%. 

Magnificent Seven enterprise software providers Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) increased 0.4% and 2%, respectively. Google wrapped up its annual developers conference on Wednesday, where it announced an array of AI innovations.  

Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) shares climbed 0.4%.

data.title: Chevron's Venezuela oil license will end next Tuesday as planned, Rubio says
data.description:
Chevron's (NYSE:CVX) U.S. license to operate in Venezuela will expire on May 27 as scheduled, Secretary of State Rubio said Wednesday night on social media, contrary to reports that the Trump administration could extend a measure that critics claim would benefit the Venezuela's Maduro regime.

"The pro-Maduro Biden oil license in #Venezuela will expire as scheduled next Tuesday, May 27," Rubio wrote in a late-night post on X.

The license, originally issued by the U.S. Treasury Department, permitted Chevron (NYSE:CVX) and certain other companies to continue working in Venezuela’s heavily sanctioned oil sector, as part of a broader effort by the Biden administration to encourage political dialogue with the Maduro government.

Shortly before Rubio's statement, representatives of the Trump administration had been quietly negotiating a deal with Venezuela that would allow Chevron (CVX) to keep exporting Venezuelan oil to the U.S. in exchange for Venezuela's acceptance of thousands of migrants expected to lose legal status in the U.S. in the coming weeks, The Miami Herald reported.

Following a trip earlier this week by U.S. special envoy Richard Grenell to Antigua, which allowed the release of an American that had been jailed for months in Venezuela, the U.S. State Department issued guidance objecting to a potential 60-day extension of the Chevron (CVX) liccense, Reuters reported.

data.title: CSW Industrials outlines fiscal 2026 revenue and EBITDA growth targets with new Aspen acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSWI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Armes, Chairman, CEO and President, announced "record results for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, adjusted earnings per diluted share, and adjusted net income for the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025." He highlighted fiscal fourth quarter revenue of $231 million, adjusted EBITDA of $60 million, adjusted earnings per diluted share of $2.24, and adjusted net income of $38 million. Armes also revealed "full-year revenue of $878 million, adjusted EBITDA of $228 million, including margin expansion of 70 basis points to 25.9%...adjusted earnings per diluted share of $8.41, adjusted net income of $137 million and cash flow from operations of $168 million."
  • Armes announced the acquisition of Aspen Manufacturing for $313.5 million, stating that "Aspen will expand our HVAC/R product offering with the addition of market leading evaporator coils and air handlers." The acquisition is described as "the second largest acquisition our company has made."
  • Armes shared, "We announced in late April, that the company will be moving to the New York Stock Exchange on June 9th. We believe this strategic move...will be beneficial to all shareholders and provide additional liquidity."
  • James Perry, EVP and CFO, reported, "We delivered record revenue of $878 million, representing growth of 11%. $38 million of the growth was organic with the remaining $48 million of growth coming from the acquisitions of Dust Free, PSP Products and PF Waterworks, that we completed since February 2024."
  • Perry noted, "Operating leverage on this revenue drove 14% growth in adjusted EBITDA, along with 70 basis points of margin expansion and over 20% growth in adjusted earnings per diluted share."

Outlook

  • Perry stated, "As we look into fiscal 2026, we anticipate delivering full-year growth in revenue and adjusted EBITDA for each segment as well as consolidated EPS growth and even stronger growth in operating cash flow than in fiscal year 2025."
  • Management expects Aspen's fiscal 2026 revenue "to grow in the high single to low-double-digits off their trailing 12-month revenue of $125 million" and Aspen's EBITDA margin "to be approximately 24% for the full fiscal year 2026."
  • "We expect Contractor Solution's overall adjusted EBITDA margin for the full fiscal year 2026 to be in the low 30s versus the recent margins closer to the mid-30s as we layer in our acquisitions and the expected impact of tariffs," Perry explained.
  • The company expects "higher full-year EBITDA margin on higher revenue" for both Specialized Reliability Solutions and Engineered Building Solutions in 2026.
  • Management stated, "We expect to see EPS growth in fiscal 2026, although the company does not anticipate EPS to grow as a percentage as much as revenue and EBITDA due to the additional shares outstanding...increased interest expense and the increased intangible amortization from recent acquisitions."

Financial Results

  • Perry reported, "Consolidated gross profit in the fiscal fourth quarter was $102 million, representing 9% growth over the prior year period. Our gross profit margin remained relatively flat at 44.2%, compared to 44.4% in the prior year period."
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $60 million with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25.9%.
  • Contractor Solutions accounted for $166 million in revenue and delivered $24.7 million or 17.5% growth versus the prior year quarter. Segment adjusted EBITDA was $56 million or 33.7% of revenue.
  • Specialized Reliability Solutions segment revenue decreased by 9% to $38 million, with segment EBITDA of $5.8 million and margin contraction to 15.3%.
  • Engineered Building Solutions segment revenue decreased by 4% to $28.7 million, with segment EBITDA of $4.2 million and margin of 14.5%.
  • Cash flow from operations for Q4 was $27 million, up 22% compared to the prior year. Free cash flow for the quarter was $22.8 million.
  • Perry highlighted that "we used most of our cash on hand at that time and borrowed $135 million from our revolving credit facility to fund the Aspen acquisition on May 1st."
  • The company renewed, extended, and upsized its revolving credit facility to $700 million.

Q&A

  • Jon Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Asked about the impact of tariffs on COGS, especially regarding Vietnam. Perry responded that "Tariffs is obviously very dynamic...there's still a 10% tariff everywhere and 30% plus in China...We expect for fiscal '26 for that number to be at/or a little less than 10%." Aspen's US-based operations are expected to mitigate some of this exposure.
  • Tanwanteng questioned M&A pipeline and revolver usage. Armes indicated a "very disciplined approach" to acquisitions, with the revolver available for larger deals. He noted a "robust and strong" target list for acquisitions but would likely wait for international manufacturing uncertainty to settle before further action.
  • Tanwanteng asked about Aspen's margin accretion. Perry said, "From an EBITDA accretion perspective, it's accretive day one from a dollar perspective, but the margins on consolidated, we think we will get there in the kind of near to mid-term."
  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs: Asked about tariff exposure as a percent of COGS. Perry clarified, "That's on COGS and that is specific to Contractor Solutions."
  • Maklari inquired about pricing actions and margin implications. Perry explained, "The price increase...will take place likely during this quarter...we think we're matching that pretty well with pricing as we need to."
  • Maklari asked about inventory and demand for the busy spring and summer season. Perry responded that "things feel relatively normal" and noted that pull forward on equipment due to regulation changes "just hasn't affected us a whole lot."
  • Sam Reed, Wells Fargo: Asked about Aspen margin guidance and swings. Perry said Aspen's margins could fluctuate "several hundred basis-points either side of that 24%."
  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities: Asked about demand trends into April/May. Perry stated that Specialized Reliability Solutions had a soft fourth quarter but improved as they moved into the new quarter. Contractor Solutions organic growth is expected to remain healthy with "mid to high single digit growth" over the long term.
  • Natalia Bak, Citi: Asked about timing to achieve 20% EBITDA margin for Engineered Building Solutions. Perry said, "20% is still the intermediate term hurdle...Are we there for the full year fiscal '26? Probably a little early to say that necessarily, but we're approaching it."
  • Bak asked about Specialized Reliability Solutions margin compression and strategies for improvement. Perry cited lower volumes as the main factor and pointed to initiatives like relocating operations to Texas for cost savings and product development.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on potential margin compression from tariffs, integration of acquisitions, and expectations for organic growth, often asking detailed follow-up questions. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, with consistent congratulations for the quarter but persistent probing for clarity on risk factors and execution.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing "record results," "strong organic volume growth," and "disciplined" capital allocation. Perry’s responses conveyed confidence in pricing power and mitigation of tariff impacts, and Armes reiterated a positive long-term outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained focused on freight, tariffs, and acquisition integration, but there was an increase in questions about margin sustainability and the incremental impact of the Aspen acquisition. Management’s tone remained upbeat, with slightly increased caution about margin compression due to tariffs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced the Aspen Manufacturing acquisition and the upcoming move to the NYSE, both new developments compared to the previous quarter.
  • Margin expansion in adjusted EBITDA and revenue growth continued, but management flagged expected margin compression in Contractor Solutions for fiscal 2026 due to tariffs and integration of acquisitions.
  • Analysts shifted their focus from freight and inventory management in Q3 to tariffs, pricing actions, and acquisition integration in Q4.
  • Management’s confidence remained high, but language around tariffs and margin impacts became more cautious.
  • Specialized Reliability Solutions and Engineered Building Solutions segments faced revenue and margin headwinds in Q4, whereas both segments posted growth in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted potential margin compression in Contractor Solutions from tariffs and acquisitions, indicating, "We are anticipating an overall cost of goods sold impact from increased tariffs."
  • Perry stated, "We continue to make strategic changes to our global supply chain to minimize the impact of tariffs and other potential disruptions."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing and effectiveness of pricing actions to offset tariff impacts and the pace of margin recovery for acquired businesses.

Final Takeaway

CSW Industrials closed fiscal 2025 with record revenue and earnings, supported by organic growth and recent acquisitions. The Aspen Manufacturing acquisition was completed and is expected to contribute high single to low-double-digit revenue growth and a 24% EBITDA margin in fiscal 2026. Management projects company-wide revenue and EBITDA growth in the coming year but anticipates some margin compression in Contractor Solutions from tariffs and integration-related costs, with mitigation strategies centered on pricing actions and supply chain adjustments. The move to the NYSE and expanded credit facilities position the company for future acquisitions and growth opportunities.
